State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Intere
(916) 322-5662        322-5660          322-5901         322-6441          322-6444

May 18, 1982


Ruth B. Galanter
Ruth B. Galanter & Associates
P.O. Box 66494
Los Angeles, CA  90066

                                        Re: Advice Letter
                                            No. 82-079

Dear Ms. Galanter:

     Thank you for your request for advice concerning
Government Code Sections 87401 and 87402.1/  You have
asked whether, as a former South Coast Regional Coastal
Commissioner, you can represent an applicant for a coastal
development permit for a site for which a prior permit was
approved "in concept only" when you were a member of the
Regional Commission.

     The facts, as I understand them from your letter and
from our telephone conversation of May 17, 1982, are as
follows:

     In 1979, AVCO Community Developers applied for a
Coastal permit for a large tract of land in the South Coast
Region. The tract was divided into "areas" for purposes of
the permit. In approving the permit, the Regional
Commission required that specified conditions be met for
each area prior to development. Some of the areas were
approved "in concept" only. Area 19, which was approved in
concept only for coastal residential sites for up to 400
units, had as specific traffic requirements that "affordable
housing" be included, and that the view from the coast road
be preserved. In addition, no development was to be
allowed in Area 19 until a subsequent, permit application

_________________
1/ All statutory references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise noted.

Ruth B. Galanter
May 18, 1982
Page 2


     for a specific proposed development was submitted and
approved.

     Because of its "in concept" approval, the AVCO permit
was, in many respects, more like a local coastal plan more
than an ordinaly coastal development permit. To this
extent, the AVCO permit was unique. It was issued in this
manner because of the large size of the AVCO tract. The
"in concept" approval for designated areas was intended to
enable AVCO either to plan to develop those areas or to
sell the property to other developers. With the AVCO
permit, another developer could know what type of use would
be allowed in the area.

     Area 19 has been sold to another developer. It is this
developer who has asked you for representation before the
Regional Commission. The permit application will be for
400 units of housing, 300 of them "affordable," as required
by the AVCO permit. The permit applications will cover
details of building design, siting, parking, layout,
building height, utilities, soil studies, sewers and other
items, none of which were issues during the 1979 AVCO
permit application. It will also include county approval,
a subdivision map, and specific provisions for protecting
the view from the coast road. The Regional Commission's
decision on the developer's permit will thus be made on the
basis of considerations which are almost totally different
(as well as much more specific) from those which formed the
basis of the 1979 permit decision.

     Section 87401 provides:

          No former state administrative official, after the
     termination of his or her employment or term of office,
     shall for compensation act as agent or attorney for, or
     otherwise represent, any other person (other than the
     State of California) before any court or state
     administrative agency or any officer or employee
     thereof:

          (a) By making any formal or informal appearance,
     or by making any oral or written communication with the
     intent to influence, in connection with any judicial,
     quasi-judicial or other proceeding if;

Ruth B. Galanter
May 18, 1982
Page 3


     (b) The State of California is a party or has a
direct and substantial interest; and

     (c) If the proceeding is one in which the former
state administrative official participated.

Section 87402 provides:

     No former state administrative official, after the
termination of his or her employment or term of office
shall for compensation aid, advise, counsel, consult or
assist in representing any other person (except the
State of California) in any proceeding in which the
official would be prohibited from appearing under
Section 87401.

You are clearly a "former state administrative
official," and a coastal permit application is and
clearly the type of proceeding covered by these sections.
Section 87400. However, it is our conclusion, on the basis
of the facts that you have presented, that the current
permit application for development of Area 19 is not the
same "proceeding" as the one in which you participated as a
regional commissioner.

As a general rule, the Fair Political Practices
Commission interprets the word "proceeding," in connection
with the Coastal Commission, to mean a specific permit
application, including all the procedural stages involved
in the application. A new permit application, even if it
involves the same tract of land, or some of the same
issues, as a prior application, is ordinarily considered a
new proceeding.

There may, however, be situations in which a new permit
application would be considered part of the same proceeding
as a prior application. Whenever both the same specific
parcel of land and the same specific issues are involved in
two permit applications, we would be inclined to advise
that the two applications constitute only one proceeding.
For example, if a permit application for a specific project
is withdrawn because it fails to satisfy some technical
requirement, and a new permit application is submitted
correcting the technical defect, the two applications would
be considered part of only one proceeding.

Ruth B. Galanter
May 18, 1982
Page 4


     In the situation you describe with respect to Area 19,
although the land parcel is the same as in the prior permit
application, the issues are almost completely different.
Thus, we conclude that you can represent the developer of
Area 19 without violating Sections 87401 or 87402.

     I understand from our telephone conversation that you
are no longer seeking advice with respect to Area 1. If,
however, you are asked to represent the developer of that
area, you should apply the same sort of analysis that I
have applied to your request on Area 19. If you have any
further questions, you should, of course, feel free to
contact me.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

BAM:pln

Ruth B. Galanter and Associates    P.O. Box 66494                    213/398-6693
                    MAY 3  0 12 AM 82        Los Angeles, CA 90066

                                            April 30, 1982


Mr. Robert Stern
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Stern:

     I am writing pursuant to §83114(b) of the Government Code to
request your advice on the relevance of §§87401 and 87402 of the
Government Code to the following situation.

     I served on the South Coast Regional Commission from January
1977 through June 1981. As a Commissioner, I voted on P-79-5539,
an application by AVCO Community Developers for a coastal permit.
That permit covered an enormous amount of land, approving some
parts for development and other parts in concept only. Those parts
approved in concept only require separate permits for actual development.

     I have been approached to represent two different applicants
for two of the necessary development permits for sites approved in
concept only in 1979. In one case (site 19 in the enclosed memo),
the current applicant is a new landowner; in the other (site 1),
the landowner is the original landowner.

     It seems to me that the issue under §87401 is whether these 1982
permit applications are part of the same proceeding I participated in
in 1979. My reading of the 1979 permit is that they are not, since a
completely new permit application is required. This contrasts with
the situation of a permit approved subject to conditions which issues
without a new application once the conditions have been met. Thus I
do not see a conflict of interest here, but I would very much appreciate your advice before I proceed further.

     I would be happy to supply any additional information you feel
would be useful for your review. As I must get back to these appli-
cants quickly, I would be grateful for your prompt attention to my
problem.

     Thank you for your help.

                                            Sincerely yours,

                                            Ruth Galanter
                                            Ruth Galanter

Enc.

STATE OF CALIFORNIA

Memorandum

September 28, 1979


Memo To:         M.J.C. and R.G.C.

From:            E.B. eb

Subject:         Future Actions Outlined Under Conditions of
                 P-5539 (Avco Community Developers).

Under the conditions imposed by the Regional Commission on the approval,
partially "in concept", of P-5539 (Avco Community Developers), the subject
permit can be issued after only one small condition is met. The conditions
specify that certain project elements (listed below) will require a subsequent coastal permit as well as require the applicant to enter into several
binding agreements (also outlined below) upon issuance of this permit (P-5539).
By accepting this permit the applicant also agrees to submit necessary plans
and associated documents for those elements not requiring additional permits
to us prior to improvement or construction of the elements. A quick synopsis
of what's required by the conditions:

1.  Prior to issuance of P-5539:
     a. signing for the community park

2.  Needs additional permit:
     a. hotel (Area 1 on application)
     b. recreation/conference center (Area 2)
     c. commercial center expansion (Area 3)
     d. coastal residential sites (Areas 7, 8, 19)

3.  Plans (for details see conditions, attached) "prior to improvement or
     construction"
     a. coastal park (Area 4)
     b. community park (Area 5)
     c. golf course (esp. Salt Creek restoration) and deed restriction (Area 6)
     d. residential areas (Areas 9 through 16 and 18)
     e. grading and runoff controls

4.  Upon issuance of P-5539 applicant will enter into following agreements:
     a. offer to dedicate coastal park (Area 4)
     b. offer to dedicate community park (Area 5)
     c. low/moderate housing agreement

5.  Prior to any sewer hookups applicant will submit money to the "Coastal
     Access Fund"; presumably this will be set up by that time and I also
     presume the designers of the fund (Bill Boyd, et. al.) will take care
     of setting it up.

DEPARTMENT OF GENERAL SERVICES

OFFICE OF ADMINISTRATIVE HEARINGS
717 K STREET, SUITE 409
SACRAMENTO 95814     445-4926

100 VAN NESS, 11TH FLOOR
SAN FRANCISCO 94102

314 WEST FIRST STREET
LOS ANGELES 90012

November 5, 1980


Mr. Willard A. Shank
Chief Assistant Attorney General
Division of Civil Law
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 390
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Shank:

RE:  1980 Statutes, Chapter 66--Effects on O.A.H. Staff

I need an informal opinion on the effect of 1980 Statutes, Chapter 66 (1979-80 AB 1048, N. Waters), on employees of the Office of Administrative Hearings who leave state service after the new law's effective date. Since the bill is effective January 1, 1981, and is retroactive, I need the opinion as soon as possible and hope you can respond on an expedited basis. If the bill prohibits or limits continuation of the professional careers of O.A.H. employees in private employment, the O.A.H. employees should know about the prohibitions in time (i.e. before year end) to make decisions about their careers.

Simply put, does the bill create a limitation or prohibition beyond matters involving a specific party or parties in which the employee officially participated? To illustrate my inquiry, is an Administrative Law Judge of the Office of Administrative Hearings prohibited from practicing law before any Government Code Section 11501 agency tribunal? Is the prohibition limited to cases pending in or previously heard by the Office of Administrative Hearings? When, if ever, does the limitation of prohibition terminate? If the prohibition is limited to specific parties, how are they identified? Does the prohibition affect Hearing Reporters, the Director or Deputy Director of the Office of Administrative Hearings, or the Director's Executive Secretary?

Once again, it is crucial that incumbent O.A.H. employees have counsel on this new law in time to make profound career decisions. Therefore, your prompt response is highly desirable and greatly appreciated.

Best wishes,

HERBERT W. DOBRICA, Director

HWD:ap

State of California
Memorandum

To : Herbert W. Nobriga, Director
     Office of Administrative Hearings
     717 K Street, Suite 409
     Sacramento, CA 95814

Date    December 10, 1980

File No.

From : Office of the Attorney General
                                        DEC 2 1980
                                        
                                        1819 101112131415

Subject: ANALYSIS OF CHAPTER 66, STATUTES OF 1980

Your request for informal advice relating to the affect
of Chapter 66, Statutes of 1980, upon employees of the Office of
Administrative Hearings has been referred to me for response.
What follows is my general analysis as to when and in what circum-
stances the prohibitions of Chapter 66 apply. Before commencing
such an analysis, however, I must caution that this memorandum is
not an opinion of the Office of the Attorney General and should
not be cited or relied upon as such.

Chapter 66 prohibits designated activity in conjunction
with judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceedings by former
state administrative officials who participated in such proceed-
ings while in state service. (See Govt. Code §§ 8240 & 8740.)
Some understanding of the extent and application of these prohi-
bitions can be gleaned from the statutory definitions of their
critical terms.

The prohibitions apply only to former "state administra-
tive officials". This term is defined in Government Code Section
87400, subdivision (b) to include persons who, as part of their
official responsibility, engage in judicial, quasi-judicial, or
other proceedings in other than a purely clerical, secretarial,
ministerial fashion. The question of whether a particular indivi-
dual is or was so engaged as part of his or her official responsi-
bilities necessitates a factual inquiry well beyond the scope of
this memorandum. However, with regard to the positions identified
in your memorandum, one can reasonably conclude that a hearing
reporter and an executive secretary typically are not state adminis-
trative officials.

If a former state administrative official is involved
it must next be determined if a "judicial, quasi-judicial or other
proceeding" existed in which the official "participated". The
term "judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" is defined in
Government Code Section 87400, subdivision (c). Pertinent to
your apparent concerns is the language limiting the term to parti

Herbert W. Nobri                                -2-                                cember 10, 1980

cular matters "involving a specific party or parties in any court
or state administrative agency". Thus, unless the matter focuses
upon a specific party or parties, it is not a judicial, quasi-
judicial, or other proceeding within the meaning of Chapter 66,
and participation therein is of no legal significance. The term
"specific party or parties" is not defined in Chapter 66, and I
hesitate to speculate as to how it may hereafter be interpreted
by the courts or this office.

If a former administrative official is involved and if
the judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding existed, the
remaining inquiry is whether the official "participated" in the
proceeding while in state service. The term "participated" is
defined in Government Code Section 8740O, subdivision (d). It
is noteworthy that participation is defined to require personal and
substantial involvement by the state administrative official in
the judicial, quasi, judicial, or other proceeding. The question
of whether a particular individual was personally and substantially
involved in such a proceeding requires a factual determination
also beyond the scope of this memorandum.

If the prohibitions apply, the only time limitation upon
their duration is set forth in Government Code Section 87403,
subdivision (c). This prohibition relies to proceedings in which
the state administrative agency has rendered a final decision,
but has also retained jurisdiction over the matter. In such
instances, the former state administrative official is excepted
from the prohibitions upon a finding by the agency that five (5)
years have elapsed since the official's separation from state
service and that the public interest would not be harmed by his
or her involvement in the proceeding.

It is my hope that the above analysis will provide some
counsel to you and other concerned Office of Administrative Hearings
employees. However, I must reiterate that the above reflects
solely the views of the undersigned and that this memorandum should
not be cited or relied upon as an official opinion of the Office
of the Attorney General.

If you have any further questions or if you otherwise
desire to discuss this matter, please feel free to telephone me at
445-8208.


M. Anthony Soares
Deputy Attorney General

MAS/ag

STATE OF CALIFORNIA

OFFICE OF LEGISLATIVE COUNSEL

Sacramento, California
December 3, 1979


Honorable Walter M. Ingalls
4016 State Capitol

Conflict of Interest: State Administrative
Officials (A.B. 1045) - #16810

Dear Mr. Ingalls:

QUESTION NO. 1

With respect to Section 67400, as it would be
added to the Government Code by Assembly Bill No. 1045, as
amended in Senate August 22, 1979, do the research and
analysis of issues regarding a particular controversy being
considered by a state administrative agency constitute
"administrative action"?

OPINION NO. 1

Research and analysis of issues regarding a
particular controversy being considered by a state administrative agency may constitute "administrative action" as
that term is used in Section 67400, provided such research
or analysis is necessary to find the facts or determine the
legal issues which, under the law, justify a particular
course of action; and provided further that such research or
analysis is not engaged in in a purely clerical or secretarial
capacity.

Honorable Walter H. Ingalls - p. 2 - #16810

ANALYSIS NO. 1

A.B. 1048, among other things, would restrict the
right of a former state administrative official to represent
for compensation any person, other than the state of California,
in specified proceedings if the proceding is one in which
the former official participated while employed by the
state.

Section 87400, as it would be added to the Government
Code* by A.B. 1048, would provide as follows:

"§7400. Unless the contrary is stated
or clearly appears from the context, the
definitions set forth in this section shall
govern the interpretation of this article.

"(a) 'state administrative agency'
means every state office, department,
division, bureau, board and commission,
but does not include the Legislature,
the courts or any agency in the judicial
branch of government.

"(b) 'state administrative official'
means every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state administrative agency
who as part of his or her official respon-
sibilities engages in any administrative
action in other than a purely clerical,
secretarial or ministerial capacity.

"(c) 'Judicial, quasi-judicial or
other proceeding' means any proceding,
application, request for a ruling or other
determination, contract, claim, controversy,
investigation, charge, accusation, arrest
or other particular matter involving a
specific party or parties in any court or
state administrative agency, including but
not limited to any proceeding governed by
Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 11500) of
Division 3 of Title 2 of the Government Code.

* All section references, unless otherwise noted, are to
sections which would be added to the Government Code by
A.B. 1048, as amended in Senate August 22, 1979.

Honorable Walter M. Ingalls - p. 3 - §16810


"(d) 'Participated' means to have
taken part personally and substantially
through decision, approval, disapproval,
formal written recommendation, rendering
advice on a substantial basis, investiga-
tion or use of confidential information
as an officer or employee, but excluding
approval, disapproval or rendering of
legal advisory opinions to departmental
or agency staff which do not involve a
specific party or parties." (Emphasis
added.)

Section 87400, above, does not define the term
"administrative action." However, we are not without
guidance as to the meaning to be given the term "administrative
acts." This phrase is defined in McKovitt v. City of Sacramento,
55 Cal. App. 117, 124, as follows:

"Acts which are to be deemed as acts
of administration, and classed among those
governmental powers properly assigned to
the executive department, are those which
are necessary to be done to carry out le-
gislative policies and purposes already
declared by the legislative body, or such
as are devolved upon it by the organic
law of its existence."

The above definition was later quoted with approval
in the case of In re Mchonough, 27 Cal. App. 2d 155, 158,
and in Redevelopment Agency v. City of Berkeley, 80 Cal.
App. 3d 158, 167.

Acts of administration are, necessarily, those
acts performed by administrative agencies; and "research
and "analysis" may be among such acts. "Research" is defined
by Webster (Webster's New Third International Dictionary) as
a "studious inquiry or investigation;" (p. 1930) and analysis
as, "a detailed examination of anything complex" (p. 77).
As the court noted in Whitten v. California State Board of
Optometry, 8 Cal. 2d 444, 446, agencies engaged in making

Honorable Walter A. Ingalls - p. 4 - 816610

administrative determinations have the power and facilities
to investigate and initiate action and to amend the law
and to find the facts which under the law justify a cause
of action. It is entirely conceivable, then, that research
and analysis, as part of the ascertainment of the course of
action required by the law, may be subsumed under the more
inclusive phrase "administrative action."

We do not exclude here the possibility that legal
research and analysis, i.e., the determination of legal
issues as well as factual issues, could also be subsumed
under the phrase "administrative action." Rather, in view
of the fact that the course of administrative action is
oftentimes guided by the determination of legal issues, we
think that the determination of legal issues would be covered
by the phrase administrative action, as used in A.B. 1946.
This conclusion is supported, in our opinion, by the provisions of subdivision (4) of Section 87400, which excludes
from the definition of the term "participated" the rendering of legal advisory opinions to departmental or agency
staff which do not involve a specific party or parties. The
implication of the wording of this exception is that,
conversely, where an attorney has rendered advice concerning
a specific party or parties, he will be declared to have
participated in a proceeding.

We note here that Section 87400 defines "state
administrative official" as one who engages in any administrative action in other than a "purely clerical, ministerial or ministerial capacity." The use of the term "ministerial"
in this context is somewhat confusing because "administrative
acts" are frequently characterized as "ministerial" note
(see Whitten, supra, p. 445). Thus it provides no guidance
at all in a quest to ascertain the meaning of "state administrative official" to refer to one who engages in
administrative acts in other than a "ministerial" capacity.
It must be presumed, therefore, that instead of such a non
sequitur, the drafters of this language meant the term
"ministerial" as used in this context to take a meaning
similar to the accompanying terms "clerical" and "secretarial,
applying the rule of construction embodied in the Latin
phrase, noscitur a sociis (literally: it is known from its
associates). This maxim of statutory construction stands
for the rule that the meaning of a word may be known from
the accompanying words (Black's Law Dictionary, 5th Edition,
p. 956).

Honorable Walter H. Ingalls - p. 5 - 916810

In conclusion, in our opinion, research and analysis of issues regarding a particular controversy being considered by a state administrative agency may constitute "administrative action" as that term is used in Section 07400, provided such research and analysis find necessary to find facts to determine the legal issues which, under the law, justify a particular course of action; and provided further that such research and analysis is not engaged in in a purely clerical or secretarial capacity.

QUESTION NO. 2

Does a state administrative agency's employee who undertakes research and analysis become a "state administrative official"?

OPINION AND ANALYSIS NO. 2

As set forth in subdivision (b) of Section 87400, supra, "state administrative official" means every employee of a state administrative agency, as defined, who as part of his or her official responsibilities engages in any administrative action in other than a purely clerical or secretarial capacity. As we have concluded in Opinion and Analysis No. 1, the research and analysis of issues regarding a particular controversy being considered by a state administrative agency may constitute "administrative action" if such research or analysis or determination, undertaken in other than a purely clerical or secretarial capacity, is necessary to find the facts or determine the legal issues which, under the law, justify a particular course of action.

Thus, if a state administrative agency's employee undertakes such research or analysis or determination, in our opinion he or she would be a "state administrative official" as that term is used in subdivision (b) of Section 87400.

QUESTION NO. 3

Is a member of a state board or commission a "state administrative official" according to the definitions contained in Section 07400?

Honorabl. Walter H. Ingalls - p. 5 - 11/6/16


OPINION NO. 3

Under the definitions contained in Section 1?:?90
"state administrative agency" includes every state board and
commission (subd. (a), Sec. 07:00); and "state administrutive
official" includes every officer of a state adminstrative
agency who as part of his or her official responsibilities
engages in any "administrative action," except in a purely
clerical or secretarial capacity (subd. (b), Sec. 07:04).

Thus, if a member of a state board or commission
engages in any administrative action, other than in a purely
clerical or secretarial capacity, he or she would be a "state
administative official" under the definitions found in
Section C?:00.


OPINION NO. 4

If a state administrative official participates
in a proceeding while employed by a state administrative
agency, could the former official not as an agent or attorney
for a party before a state court or a state administative
agency with respect to such proceeding prior to the expiration of five years?


OPINION NO. 5

A state administrative official who participated
in a proceeding while employed by a state administrative agency
cannot act as an agent or attorney for a party before a state
court or a state administrative agency with respect to such
proceeding prior to the expiration of five years.


EXAMPLE NO. 1

Section C?:01 provides as follows:

"C?:01. No former state administra-
tive official, after the termination of
his or her employment or term of office,
shall for compensation act as agent or

Honorable Walter H. Ingalls - p. 7 - [illegible]

attorney for, or otherwise represent,
any other person (other than the State
of California) before any court or state
administrative agency or any officer or
employee thereof.

"(d) by mailing any form or in-
formal appearance, or by filing any oral
or written communication with the intent
to influence, in connection with any judi-
cial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding
it.

"(e) The State of California is a
party or has a direct and substantial
interest; and

"(f) If the proceeding is one in
which the former state administrative
official participated."

and adds prohibition, is not limited as to when, however, fiction U703 [illegible] the enumeration shall
not apply in certain instances. In particular, subdivision
(c) of Section U703 provides as follows:

"U703.    * * *

"(c) With respect to any appearance
or communication in a proceeding in
which a court or state administrative
agency has issued a final order, decree,
decision, or judgment and the jurisdiction is the state administrative
agency or former employment gave its
consent by determining that:

"(1) At least five years have
elapsed since the termination of the
former state official's official's
employment or term of office; and

"(2) the public interest would
not be harmed."

Honorable Wilson H. Dosulla - p. 6 - 11G610

"Thus, an exception to the prohibition contained
in Section 6702 arises in the limited situation where a
court or administrative agency has found a third party
degree, decision or judgment but has retained jurisdiction
if the state administrative agency of that State approves it as
its consent by determining that (1) at least five years have
elapsed since the termination of the former state adjudica-
tive official's employment or term of office; and (2) the
public interest would not be harmed.

Conversely, we think it is clear that in cases
which do not come within this exception (e.g., where less
than five years have elapsed since a former state administra-
tive official cannot act as an agent or attorney in a pro-
ceeding before that state judicial official while employed in
the state.

QUESTION 5

Are there any provisions before a "state
administrative agency" in the state of California
"is not a party of one not have a direct and substantial
interest?

OPINION NO. 5

Any proceeding in which a state administrative
agency would be one in which the state of California is
a party or has a direct and substantial interest.

ANSWER NO. 5

Section 4703, et seq. above, although in language
similar to that suggested by earlier Opinions, makes it clear
that a proceeding before a court or state administrative
agency before a court where a state administrative agency of
the state of California is a party or, in the cases where the
state of California is not a party, where the state has
direct and substantial interest.

Honorable Walter N. Ensall - p. 5 - 213819

The term "Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" as used in Section 3749.1 is defined in subdivision (c) of Section 3749.5 as follows:

"3749.5.   ' & &

"(c) 'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, mediation, arbitration or any ruling or other examination, contempt, etc., controversy, investigation, charge, inquest, grand or other inquiry or matter involving a party or parties in any court of state or tribunal matters, [illegible] back not limited to any proceeding governed by Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 11500) of Division 3 of Title 2 of the Government Code." (Italics added.)

In case to state the test other investigating or used in substitution (c) of Section 3749.5, there is [illegible] broad administrative scope. The statute specifically includes proceedings taken by state against (see Sec. 11500, Gov. C.) under the administrative regulation provisions of the Administrative Procedure Act in this category (see Ch. 5 (commencing with Sec. 11500), Div. 3 (§11500, et seq. C.). However, Section 3749.1 is not limited to only those proceedings.

Any such proceeding under the Administrative Procedure Act would, of course, be a proceeding where an agency of the state of California would be a party. "Other proceeding" not covered by the Administrative Procedure Act, for example, those of the Public Utilities Commission (see Sec. 11445, Rev. C.), would remain subject to Section 3749.1 proceedings in which the state did not and has a direct and substantial interest. In other cases, where the state is not traceable a party, it appears that it would nevertheless have a direct and substantial interest in the outcome of the proceeding because other statutory provisions would not have authorized the state's role in the proceeding. For example, under Section 88 of the

Renonville Water H. Duselle—p. 30—216619

Labor Code the Labor Commissioner may provide for a
hearing before the court, in an action to recover
wages, penalties and other amounts owing for per-

his jurisdiction. While the state is not a party to

sections.question: You have asked whether, as a former South Coast Regional Coastal Commissioner, you can represent an applicant for a coastal development permit for a site for which a prior permit was approved 'in concept only' when you were a member of the Regional Commission.
sections.conclusion: It is our conclusion, on the basis of the facts that you have presented, that the current permit application for development of Area 19 is not the same 'proceeding' as the one in which you participated as a regional commissioner. Although the land parcel is the same as in the prior permit application, the issues are almost completely different. Thus, we conclude that you can represent the developer of Area 19 without violating Sections 87401 or 87402.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a former South Coast Regional Coastal Commissioner represent a developer seeking a permit for an area that was previously approved 'in concept only' under a 1979 AVCO permit that the commissioner voted on, without violating Government Code Sections 87401 and 87402?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the 1982 permit application for Area 19 constitutes a new proceeding distinct from the 1979 AVCO permit vote because the issues are substantially different, despite involving the same parcel. Therefore, the former commissioner may represent the new developer without violating the post-employment restrictions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Well-structured advice letter with clear question, facts, legal analysis, and conclusion sections. Some scanned document quality issues in ancillary materials but main letter is clear. Letter includes incoming request from May 1982 and FPPC response from May 18, 1982.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have asked whether, as a former South Coast Regional Coastal Commissioner, you can represent an applicant for a coastal development permit for a site for which a prior permit was approved 'in concept only' when you were a member of the Regional Commission.

CONCLUSION: It is our conclusion, on the basis of the facts that you have presented, that the current permit application for development of Area 19 is not the same 'proceeding' as the one in which you participated as a regional commissioner. Although the land parcel is the same as in the prior permit application, the issues are almost completely different. Thus, we conclude that you can represent the developer of Area 19 without violating Sections 87401 or 87402.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Intere (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 May 18, 1982 Ruth B. Galanter Ruth B. Galanter & Associates P.O. Box 66494 Los Angeles, CA 90066 Re: Advice Letter No. 82-079 Dear Ms. Galanter: Thank you for your request for advice concerning Government Code Sections 87401 and 87402.1/ You have asked whether, as a former South Coast Regional Coastal Commissioner, you can represent an applicant for a coastal development permit for a site for which a prior permit was approved "in concept only" when you were a member of the Regional Commission. The facts, as I understand them from your letter and from our telephone conversation of May 17, 1982, are as follows: In 1979, AVCO Community Developers applied for a Coastal permit for a large tract of land in the South Coast Region. The tract was divided into "areas" for purposes of the permit. In approving the permit, the Regional Commission required that specified conditions be met for each area prior to development. Some of the areas were approved "in concept" only. Area 19, which was approved in concept only for coastal residential sites for up to 400 units, had as specific traffic requirements that "affordable housing" be included, and that the view from the coast road be preserved. In addition, no development was to be allowed in Area 19 until a subsequent, permit application _________________ 1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted. Ruth B. Galanter May 18, 1982 Page 2 for a specific proposed development was submitted and approved. Because of its "in concept" approval, the AVCO permit was, in many respects, more like a local coastal plan more than an ordinaly coastal development permit. To this extent, the AVCO permit was unique. It was issued in this manner because of the large size of the AVCO tract. The "in concept" approval for designated areas was intended to enable AVCO either to plan to develop those areas or to sell the property to other developers. With the AVCO permit, another developer could know what type of use would be allowed in the area. Area 19 has been sold to another developer. It is this developer who has asked you for representation before the Regional Commission. The permit application will be for 400 units of housing, 300 of them "affordable," as required by the AVCO permit. The permit applications will cover details of building design, siting, parking, layout, building height, utilities, soil studies, sewers and other items, none of which were issues during the 1979 AVCO permit application. It will also include county approval, a subdivision map, and specific provisions for protecting the view from the coast road. The Regional Commission's decision on the developer's permit will thus be made on the basis of considerations which
embedding.summary: FPPC Advice Letter 82-079 addresses whether a former South Coast Regional Coastal Commissioner can represent a developer in a 1982 coastal permit application for an area approved 'in concept only' in a 1979 AVCO permit on which the commissioner voted. The FPPC advises that the applications constitute separate proceedings due to substantially different issues, permitting the representation without violating Sections 87401 and 87402.
